beats.prompt: Quick warm-up — match each starting state's exits to the forced complement.

pairs.left: Any state: total of its out-edges

feedback.correct: Those familiar splits — the fair coin's 1/2-1/2, the gambler's 2/5-3/5 — are exactly the **rows** of a transition matrix, and each one sums to 1.
hints: Warm-up from Penney's / Gambler's Ruin — a state's exits, no pressure.

From where you are now you go somewhere next with certainty, so a state's outgoing probabilities are a full menu that must add to 1. A row summing to 9/10 means the chain vanishes a tenth of the time; 11/10 means it's in two places at once.
Fair coin: T = 1/2; gambler: down = 3/5; any state's out-edges total 1.

beats.prompt: The Rainy row of the Clear/Rainy weather chain reads [3/10, ?]. What must the missing entry be?

options: Can't tell — it depends on the weather

7/10 — forced by the row totaling 1.note: Right — you never need the weather for the last entry. The Rainy row must total 1, so it's 1 − 3/10 = 7/10.

3/10 — the two out-edges must match.note: Out-edges only have to *sum* to 1, not match. From Rainy it's 3/10 to flip and 7/10 to stay — different numbers, same row.

Can't tell — it depends on the weather.note: You never need the story for the LAST entry — the row must total 1, so it's forced: ? = 1 − 3/10 = 7/10.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit and we'll check the row total.
The last entry never needs the story — the row must total 1, so it's forced; and the two out-edges don't have to match, only sum to 1.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on the transition matrix — then continue.

interaction.title: The transition matrix
interaction.body: Stack each state's outgoing probabilities as one **row** and you get the **transition matrix** `P`. The Clear row holds Clear's exits; the Rainy row holds Rainy's. Because you always go *somewhere* next, **every row sums to 1** (row-stochastic — Green Book p.53). The transition graph (Fig 5.1) is the same object drawn as arrows.

feedback.correct: Each state's exits, stacked into a row — that's `P`, and every row sums to 1.
hints: Rows, not columns.
Row i = state i's outgoing probabilities (its arrows).
P[i][j] = the arrow i→j; every row totals 1.

beats.prompt: The Clear row is [3/5, ?]. Fill in the missing entry so the row sums to 1.

fields.label: Clear row is [3/5, ?] — fill P(Clear → Rainy)

feedback.correct: 1 − 3/5 = **2/5**. The last entry is forced by the row summing to 1 — you need nothing about the weather.
hints: The Clear row must total 1. What's left after 3/5?
You never need the story for the last entry — the row must total 1, so it's forced: ? = 1 − 3/5 = 2/5.

beats.prompt: Drag any edge and watch its matrix cell mirror it — a node's two arrows always re-close to 1.

hero.structuralReadout: Every arrow Clear→Rainy you set IS the cell P[Clear][Rainy]; Clear's two arrows ARE its row, and that row always closes to 1.

feedback.correct: Drag any arrow and its matrix cell moves with it; a node's two arrows always re-close to 1 — the diagram and the grid are one object.
hints: Drag an edge or step a split; watch the matrix cell mirror it.
They're one object in two outfits. Every arrow i→j labeled p IS the cell P[i][j]; all the arrows out of a node ARE that node's row. Drag an edge and the matrix moves with it.
Clear row [3/5, 2/5], Rainy row [3/10, 7/10] — each sums to 1.

beats.prompt: Reveal each of three lenses — diagram, matrix, and row-sum law — and watch them converge on one object P.

lenses.label: Diagram
lenses.body: Arrows out of each state, each labeled with a probability; one state's arrows fan out to total 1.

lenses.label: Matrix P
lenses.body: Stack each state's arrows as a row of the M×M grid P; the entry P[i][j] is exactly the arrow i→j.

lenses.label: Row-stochastic
lenses.body: Both pictures obey one law: every row of P sums to 1 — rows (a state's exits), not columns.

beats.interviewNote: Row-stochastic — every row of P sums to 1 — is the first sanity check an interviewer expects you to state about a transition matrix.

feedback.correct: Diagram, grid, and the row-sum law are three views of **one** object `P` — and it's the **rows** (each state's exits) that sum to 1, never the columns.
hints: Reveal each view — they describe the same P.

A row is ONE starting state's options, so it sums to 1. A column gathers arrows from different starting states into one destination — there's no reason that totals anything. (Row-stochastic is the Green Book convention, p.53.)
Diagram = matrix = row-stochastic: every row of P sums to 1.

beats.prompt: Not every grid is a transition matrix — match each row to its verdict.

feedback.correct: A grid is a transition matrix only if **every row sums to 1**. 3/10 + 6/10 = 9/10 fails — fix 6/10 → 7/10 to close the Rainy row.
hints: Add each row across. Which one doesn't reach 1?
Not every grid of probabilities is a P — the only test is rows-sum-to-1. A row of 3/10 + 6/10 = 9/10 leaks a tenth of the probability, so the chain isn't valid.

beats.prompt: 'Sunny stays sunny 7 in 10 times; when it rains, it turns sunny 4 in 10 times.' Build the transition matrix — each sentence fills one row.

feedback.correct: 'Sunny stays sunny 7/10' is the diagonal P[Sunny][Sunny] = 7/10, leaving 3/10 to turn rainy; 'rainy→sunny 4/10' leaves 6/10 to stay rainy. **P = [[7/10, 3/10], [4/10, 6/10]]** — both rows sum to 1.
hints: Each sentence fills one cell; the rest of that row is forced to total 1.
'Sunny stays sunny 7/10' is a real step Sunny→Sunny — it's the diagonal entry P[Sunny][Sunny] = 7/10, and it counts toward that row's sum like any other arrow.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge — build the Land-of-Oz transition matrix and answer the two-step query.

interaction.scenario: In the **Land of Oz** the weather is Rain, Nice, or Snow. They **never have two nice days in a row** — after a nice day it's a coin-flip between rain and snow. After rain or snow there's an even chance the next day is the **same**; when it does **change**, only half those changes are to a nice day (the other half swap rain↔snow). Build \(P\) (rows Rain, Nice, Snow) and verify every row sums to 1.

fields.label: Each of the three rows (Rain, Nice, Snow) sums to

feedback.correct: Land-of-Oz \(P = [[1/2,1/4,1/4],[1/2,0,1/2],[1/4,1/4,1/2]]\) (Rain/Nice/Snow): \(P(\text{Nice} \to \text{Nice}) = 0\), every row sums to 1, and two steps out \((P^2)_{\text{Rain,Snow}} = (1/2)(1/4) + (1/4)(1/2) + (1/4)(1/2) = \mathbf{3/8}\) — the exact chain L3 opens on.
hints: Each sentence sets one row; the diagonal is 'stays the same,' and a row's last entry is forced to total 1.
A third state changes nothing about the law — every row is still one starting day's full menu, so the Rain, Nice, AND Snow rows each sum to 1 (even the Nice row, which is 1/2 + 0 + 1/2).
Nice→Nice = 0; Snow→Snow = 1/2; each row sums to 1; (P²)_Rain,Snow = 3/8.

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to review the core principle.

feedback.correct: A transition matrix is the diagram rewritten: each state's exits stack into a row, and because you always go somewhere next, **every row sums to 1** — so the diagram and the matrix are one object.
hints: Every arrow i→j labeled p IS the cell P[i][j]; a node's arrows ARE its row.
A missing entry is never free — it's 1 − (the rest).
Next up: multiply P by itself — where are you after two steps? (Pⁿ).
